answer:
**Yama Smṛti (slug: unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d) does not contain rules laid down by Yama for purification after contact with the dead.**[^1]

The text consists of two chapters addressed to sages. Chapter 1 (63 paragraphs) discusses general purification methods (e.g., by water, fire, twilight rites, or bathing after touching impure substances)[^2] and prescribes penances (*kṛcchra*, *Cāndrāyaṇa*, *Aindava* vows, etc.) for sins like eating forbidden food, intercourse with prohibited persons/animals, killing, apostasy, and liquor consumption—but makes no reference to corpses, death, or contact with the dead.[^3] Chapter 2 (77 paragraphs) focuses on fines, punishments, and social penalties (e.g., for abandoning sacred fire, serving Shudras, or minor offenses), with no purification rules or mention of the dead.[^4]

Searches for terms like "purification dead," "corpse," "death," "impurity," and combinations yielded no matches in this work (though other Smṛtis like Manusmṛti ch. 5–6 do address corpse impurity).[^5]

[^1]: Full work metadata and chapters: *Yama Smṛti*, [ch.1](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/), [ch.2](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/).
[^2]: E.g., [p-65c739](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-65c739) on impure substances generally (not the dead).
[^3]: *Yama Smṛti* ch.1, [full chapter](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/).
[^4]: *Yama Smṛti* ch.2, [full chapter](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/).
[^5]: Corpus search results for Yama Smṛti-specific queries returned 0 hits.
